source: USDA Seasonal Produce Guide, local harvest data, grocery industry pricing

spring.tip: Spring greens are at their cheapest and most flavorful. Stock up on spinach and lettuce. Strawberries hit peak in late April/May — buy local.

summer.tip: Summer is produce paradise. Farmers markets have the best prices on tomatoes, corn, and berries. Buy berries in bulk and freeze them — they're 2-3x more expensive in winter. Watermelon is cheapest in July.

fall.tip: Fall root vegetables are cheap, nutritious, and last weeks in the fridge. Buy apples and pears in bulk at orchards. Sweet potatoes are cheapest in October-November. Stock up on butternut squash — stores for months.

winter.tip: Citrus is king in winter — cheapest and most nutritious. Cabbage, potatoes, carrots, and onions are cheap year-round but especially in winter. Avoid out-of-season berries ($6+ for a tiny container). Buy frozen instead.

yearRound.tip: These staples are cheap regardless of season. Build meals around them and add seasonal produce for variety and savings.

savingsTips: Buy in-season produce for 30-50% savings over out-of-season
Frozen fruits and vegetables are picked at peak ripeness and often cheaper than fresh out-of-season — nutritionally equal
Farmers markets are cheapest at end of day — vendors would rather sell cheap than take produce home
Buy ugly/imperfect produce — same nutrition, 30-50% cheaper (Misfits Market, Imperfect Foods, or in-store 'ugly produce' bins)
Whole fruits/vegetables are cheaper than pre-cut — pay for the convenience, not the food
Store-brand canned and frozen produce is nearly identical to name-brand at 30-40% less
